en.mechanism_prose: Both substances suppress the brainstem reflexes that drive breathing. Combined, the effect compounds — respiratory depression that either alone might not cause becomes lethal. Sedation also stacks, so the person stops noticing they are in trouble before breathing fails. This is the most common cause of accidental overdose deaths in the US and Europe.
warning_signs: Blue or gray lips, fingertips, or skin
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 immediately — do not wait to see if it gets better
Place them in recovery position (on their side, top knee bent, head tilted)
If naloxone (Narcan) is available, give it; repeat every 2-3 minutes if no response
Stay with them and rescue breathe if trained — call 911 back if they stop breathing

en.mechanism_prose: The stimulant masks dissociative effects, making it easy to redose the dissociative to risky levels. Stimulant-driven hyperthermia and cardiovascular load compound with dissociation-driven loss of body awareness — the person feels alert but cannot judge their own state. Hospital admissions for this combination cluster around redose accidents and overheating, not direct toxicity.
warning_signs: Hot or dry skin, rising body temperature
Loss of motor control or coordination
Rapid heartbeat that does not settle
first_aid: Move to a cool, quiet space and reduce stimulation
Hydrate slowly with water, not energy drinks
Do not give more of either substance — wait it out
Stay with them; call 911 / 112 if unresponsive or breathing changes

en.mechanism_prose: Stacking two depressants compounds central nervous system suppression. The body slows breathing, heart rate, and gag reflex in ways that either substance alone would not. The risk window is also longer than people expect: an extra drink hours after a GHB dose can tip a manageable state into unconsciousness and aspiration. Vomiting while unconscious is a common cause of death here.
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 immediately — do not assume they will sleep it off
Place them in recovery position so vomit can drain, not block airway
Stay with them and watch breathing; do not give coffee or cold showers
Keep calling 911 if breathing stops — start rescue breaths if trained

en.mechanism_prose: Alcohol and similar depressants reduce protective reflexes; dissociatives reduce body awareness and motor control. The two together produce a state where the person cannot judge their position, cannot reliably swallow, and may pass out without warning. Common harms are falls, aspiration of vomit, and respiratory depression. Cold water and bathtubs are especially dangerous in this state.
warning_signs: Falls, stumbling, or inability to stand
Slurred speech that progresses to silence
first_aid: Move them away from water, stairs, and open windows
Place in recovery position so they cannot aspirate
Stay with them — do not let them bathe or shower alone
Call 911 / 112 if unresponsive, breathing changes, or you cannot wake them

en.mechanism_prose: Both substances push serotonin levels high. Stacking them can trigger serotonin syndrome — a runaway state of hyperthermia, agitation, muscle rigidity, and clonus that can be fatal within hours. Tramadol is especially risky here because it is both an opioid and a serotonin reuptake inhibitor. Onset is sometimes delayed by several hours from the last dose, so the person may feel fine before getting worse.
warning_signs: Fast heart rate, high blood pressure
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 — serotonin syndrome is a medical emergency
Move to a cool place and remove extra clothing to reduce body temperature
Do not give any more substances, including caffeine or other stimulants
Stay with them and keep calling 911 if symptoms get worse

en.mechanism_prose: Stimulants mask the sedating effect of depressants, so people drink or dose more than they would otherwise. Cocaine plus alcohol also produces cocaethylene in the liver, a metabolite that lasts longer than cocaine and is harder on the heart. The cardiovascular risk is higher than either substance alone, and the impairment is harder to feel coming because the stimulant hides it.
warning_signs: Drinking far more than usual without feeling drunk
Chest pain, racing heart, or pounding pulse
Becoming suddenly very drunk when the stimulant wears off
first_aid: Stop drinking and sit somewhere cool and quiet
Drink water slowly — not coffee or energy drinks
Watch for chest pain or trouble breathing; call 911 / 112 if either appears
Stay with them through the comedown — the depressant hits harder when the stimulant fades

en.mechanism_prose: Two stimulants stack the load on the heart and the body's heat regulation. Heart rate, blood pressure, and body temperature climb together. The user feels alert and capable, but the cardiovascular strain is invisible from the inside. Dehydration and overheating are the most common harms; cardiac events, while rarer, can happen in people with no prior diagnosis when stimulant doses stack.
warning_signs: Chest pain, pressure, or pounding heartbeat
Skin that is hot and dry rather than sweating
Jaw clenching, jitter, or tremor that does not settle
first_aid: Move to a cool place and reduce activity — no dancing
Sip water slowly to rehydrate without flooding
Do not redose to chase the feeling — the next dose stacks the load
If chest pain, trouble breathing, or confusion appear, call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: Stacking dissociatives compounds loss of body awareness, motor control, and consciousness. The k-hole or equivalent state arrives faster and is harder to come out of. Vomiting risk is high; aspiration risk is higher because protective reflexes drop too. Long-acting dissociatives like DXM extend the window during which a second dose can stack into a much deeper state.
warning_signs: Falling, inability to stand or hold position
Loss of voice, frozen breathing pauses
first_aid: Move them to a flat, padded space — no stairs, no water
Place in recovery position to keep airway clear
Stay with them; do not let them get up alone
Call 911 / 112 if breathing pauses, vomit blocks airway, or you cannot wake them

en.mechanism_prose: Depressants blunt the body but not the head trip — the result is a person who looks fine to others while struggling internally, or one who suddenly stumbles into a worse state when the depressant peaks. Cannabis on a psychedelic peak often triggers anxiety spirals and dissociation that the trip alone would not. Alcohol increases nausea and impairs the user's ability to ground themselves.
warning_signs: Sudden anxiety, panic, or dissociation during a trip
Falling or stumbling while otherwise looking calm
Vomiting that the person cannot manage
first_aid: Move to a quiet, dim, familiar space
Speak calmly and reassure — bad trips often resolve with grounding
Hydrate slowly; offer something soft to eat
If they cannot be reached or show signs of physical distress, call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: The combination of dissociative and psychedelic effects (sometimes called candyflipping with ketamine plus LSD) can produce strong ego dissolution and disorientation that either substance alone would not. Body coordination drops while perception becomes more vivid, which raises injury risk. People often underestimate the dissociative dose because the psychedelic is doing the heavy lifting on perception. Difficult psychological experiences are common.
warning_signs: Severe disorientation or inability to locate body
Falling or being unable to stand
first_aid: Move to a flat, padded space and reduce sensory input
Stay with them and speak calmly — do not try to logically argue
Do not redose either substance
If they cannot be reached, are physically unwell, or you are scared, call 911 / 112
